---
title: "Introducing GitHub <br> `r emo::ji('octocat')`"
author: "S. Mason Garrison"
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: "../slides.css"
    lib_dir: libs
    self_contained: TRUE
    nature:
      ratio: "16:9"
      highlightLines: true
      highlightStyle: solarized-light
      countIncrementalSlides: false
---

```{r child = "../setup.Rmd"}
```

```{r packages, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Remember to compile
#xaringan::inf_mr(cast_from = "..")
knitr::opts_chunk$set(knitr.duplicate.label = "allow")
library(tidyverse)
if (!require("emo")) devtools::install_github("hadley/emo")
library(emo)
if (!require("fontawesome")) devtools::install_github("rstudio/fontawesome")
library(fontawesome)
```

class: middle

# What is GitHub?

---

## Learning Goals

By the end of this session, you will be able to...

- Explain what Git and GitHub are and why they matter
- Navigate the GitHub interface
- Create and fork a repository
- Make commits and understand the basic Git workflow
- Connect a GitHub repository to RStudio
- Use the pull → stage → commit → push cycle in your own work

---

## Version Control: The Problem It Solves

.pull-left[
Have you ever had files like this?

- `analysis_final.R`
- `analysis_final_v2.R`
- `analysis_FINAL_for_real.R`
- `analysis_FINAL_use_this_one.R`
- `analysis_actually_final.R`
]

--

.pull-right[
```{r echo=FALSE, fig.align = "center", out.width = "80%"}
knitr::include_graphics("../d02_toolkit/img/phd_comics_vc.gif")
```
]

---

## What is Git?

- **Git** is a version control system
  - Tracks changes to files over time
  - Lets you go back to any previous version
  - Like "Track Changes" in Word — but for your entire project

--

- **GitHub** is a website that hosts Git repositories
  - Cloud storage for your code and data projects
  - Adds collaboration features (comments, issues, pull requests)
  - Like Dropbox — but purpose-built for code

--

.question[
Think of Git as the engine and GitHub as the dashboard.
]

---

## Why Should Psychologists Care?

--

- **Reproducibility**: Share your exact analysis so others can reproduce it

--

- **Collaboration**: Work with co-authors without emailing files back and forth

--

- **Transparency**: Show reviewers (and your future self!) every change you made

--

- **Backup**: Never lose your work again

--

- **Portfolio**: Showcase your projects to future employers or grad programs

---

class: middle

# Key Concepts

---

## The Building Blocks of GitHub

.pull-left[
**Repository (repo)**  
A project folder tracked by Git. Contains all your files and their full history.

**Commit**  
A saved snapshot of your project at a point in time. Each commit has a message describing what changed.

**Branch**  
A parallel version of your repo. Lets you experiment without affecting the main version.
]

.pull-right[
**Fork**  
Your personal copy of someone else's repository on GitHub.

**Clone**  
Downloading a repository to your local computer.

**Pull Request (PR)**  
A proposal to merge changes from one branch (or fork) into another. The main way to collaborate.
]

---

## Visualizing Commits

```{r echo=FALSE, fig.align = "center", out.width = "70%"}
knitr::include_graphics("../d02_toolkit/img/lego-steps.png")
```

Each step = one commit. You can always go back!

---

## Commits Have Messages

```{r echo=FALSE, fig.align = "center", out.width = "70%"}
knitr::include_graphics("../d02_toolkit/img/lego-steps-commit-messages.png")
```

Good commit messages describe *why* you made a change, not just *what* changed.

---

class: middle

# Getting Started with GitHub

---

## Step 1: Create a GitHub Account

.instructions[
Go to [github.com](https://github.com) and sign up for a free account.
]

Tips for choosing a username:

- Use your real name or a professional handle — you'll share this with professors, collaborators, and future employers
- Keep it short and memorable
- Avoid numbers and special characters if possible
- You can change it later, but it's a hassle

--

.small[
If you already have an account, log in and follow along!
]

---

## Step 2: Explore the GitHub Interface

.center[
![Screenshot of the GitHub interface showing the profile, search bar, and repository list](../img/demo.png)
]

Key areas to explore:

- Your **profile** and avatar (top right)
- The **search bar** — find repos, people, code
- **Repositories** tab on your profile
- The **Explore** page for inspiration

---

## Anatomy of a Repository

A GitHub repository page shows you:

- 📁 **Files and folders** in the project
- 📝 **README** — the project's front page (written in Markdown)
- 🕐 **Commits** — the full history of changes
- 🌿 **Branches** — parallel versions of the project
- ⚙️ **Issues** — bug reports and feature requests
- 🔀 **Pull Requests** — proposed changes under review

---

class: middle

# Hands-On Activity 1

---

.your-turn[

**Explore a Real Repository**

1. Go to [github.com/hadley/r4ds](https://github.com/hadley/r4ds) — the repo for the *R for Data Science* book
2. Click the **Commits** link (near the top of the file list) to see the history
3. Click on any commit to see exactly what changed
4. Go back and click on any file to read it
5. Find the **Issues** tab — what kinds of conversations are happening?

⏱️ *Take 3 minutes to explore*

]

---

class: middle

# Your First Repository

---

## Creating a New Repository

.instructions[
On GitHub.com, click the **+** in the top right corner → **New repository**
]

Fill in:

- **Repository name**: `my-first-repo` (use hyphens, not spaces)
- **Description**: Something brief like "My first GitHub repository"
- ✅ **Add a README file** — always do this!
- **License**: Choose *MIT* for open sharing, or skip for now

Click **Create repository** 🎉

---

## Editing Files on GitHub.com

You can edit files directly in the browser — great for quick fixes!

1. Click on `README.md` in your new repo
2. Click the **pencil icon** ✏️ (Edit this file)
3. Add a line: `This is my first GitHub repository!`
4. Scroll down to **Commit changes**
5. Write a commit message: `Add description to README`
6. Click **Commit changes**

--

.question[
What just happened? You made your first commit! 🎉
]

---

class: middle

# Forking and Cloning

---

## Forking: Getting Your Own Copy

**Forking** creates a copy of someone else's repository in your own GitHub account.

Use forking when you want to:
- Use someone's project as a starting point
- Contribute to an open-source project
- Complete a class assignment

--

.instructions[
Go to a classmate's (or the instructor's) repository and click **Fork** in the top right.
]

---

## Cloning: From GitHub to Your Computer

**Cloning** downloads a repository to your local machine so you can work on it in RStudio.

```
git clone https://github.com/YOUR-USERNAME/REPO-NAME.git
```

But we'll use RStudio's interface — no command line needed!

---

## Clone a Repo in RStudio

.instructions[
**File → New Project → Version Control → Git**
]

1. Copy the repository URL from GitHub (green **Code** button → HTTPS)
2. Paste it in the **Repository URL** box in RStudio
3. Choose where to save it on your computer
4. Click **Create Project**

RStudio will download the repo and open it as a project. You'll see a **Git** tab in the upper-right pane.

---

class: middle

# The Basic Git Workflow

---

## Pull → Work → Stage → Commit → Push

.center[
.large[
`r emo::ji("arrow_down")` **Pull** → ✏️ **Work** → ✅ **Stage** → `r emo::ji("floppy_disk")` **Commit** → `r emo::ji("arrow_up")` **Push**
]
]

<br>

| Step | What it does | Where in RStudio |
|------|-------------|-----------------|
| Pull | Get latest changes | Blue ↓ arrow |
| Work | Edit your files | Editor pane |
| Stage | Select files to save | Check ✅ boxes in Git tab |
| Commit | Save a snapshot | Commit button + message |
| Push | Send to GitHub | Green ↑ arrow |

---

## The Workflow, Step by Step

**1. Pull** — Get the latest changes from GitHub before you start working  
`r emo::ji("arrow_down")` Blue down arrow in RStudio's Git tab

--

**2. Work** — Make changes to your files as usual

--

**3. Stage** — Tell Git which changes to include in the next snapshot  
✅ Check the boxes next to files in the Git tab

--

**4. Commit** — Save a snapshot with a descriptive message  
`r emo::ji("floppy_disk")` Click Commit, write your message, click Commit

--

**5. Push** — Send your commits up to GitHub  
`r emo::ji("arrow_up")` Green up arrow in RStudio's Git tab

---

## Writing Good Commit Messages

.pull-left[
**Bad commit messages** 😬

- `stuff`
- `fix`
- `asdfgh`
- `final`
- `final final`
- `I hate this`
]

.pull-right[
**Good commit messages** ✅

- `Add demographic summary table`
- `Fix typo in methods section`
- `Remove outliers with z-score > 3`
- `Update figure 2 to use color-blind palette`
- `Add bootstrap confidence intervals`
]

--

**Rule of thumb**: Complete the sentence — *"If applied, this commit will..."*

---

class: middle

# Hands-On Activity 2

---

.your-turn[

**Your First Full Git Workflow**

1. Open the project you cloned in RStudio
2. Click the blue **Pull** arrow in the Git tab (get latest)
3. Open `README.md` and add your name and a fun fact about yourself
4. Save the file
5. In the Git tab, check ✅ the box next to `README.md` to stage it
6. Click **Commit**, write a message like `Add my name and fun fact`
7. Click **Commit**, then click the green **Push** arrow
8. Go to GitHub and refresh the page — your changes are live! 🎉

⏱️ *Take 5 minutes*

]

---

class: middle

# Collaborating on GitHub

---

## Issues: Tracking Tasks and Bugs

**Issues** are GitHub's built-in to-do list and bug tracker.

Use them to:
- Report a bug you found
- Request a new feature
- Ask a question about the project
- Assign tasks to collaborators

Every issue gets a number (`#1`, `#2`, etc.) and can be referenced in commit messages:  
`Fix color palette, closes #7`

---

## Pull Requests: Proposing Changes

A **Pull Request (PR)** is how you propose merging changes from one branch or fork into another.

The workflow:
1. Fork the repo (or create a branch)
2. Make your changes
3. Push to GitHub
4. Click **New Pull Request** on GitHub
5. Describe your changes and request a review
6. Collaborators review, discuss, request changes
7. PR gets merged (or closed)

---

## Branching: Working in Parallel

**Branches** let multiple people (or you!) work on different features simultaneously without conflicts.

Common branch strategy:
- `main` — stable, production-ready code
- `feature/new-analysis` — work-in-progress
- `fix/typo-in-methods` — a quick fix

.small[
For most class projects, you won't need branches — `main` is fine!
]

---

class: middle

# GitHub Tips and Tricks

---

## GitHub in RStudio: Quick Reference

| Action | Where in RStudio |
|--------|-----------------|
| Pull latest changes | Git tab → Blue ↓ arrow |
| Stage files | Git tab → Check ✅ boxes |
| Commit | Git tab → Commit button |
| Push to GitHub | Git tab → Green ↑ arrow |
| View history | Git tab → Clock 🕐 icon |
| Diff (see changes) | Git tab → Diff button |

---

## When Things Go Wrong

**"I can't push!"**  
→ Try pulling first. You may need to merge someone else's changes.

**"My files aren't showing up in the Git tab"**  
→ Make sure you're working inside the RStudio Project (`.Rproj` file).

**"I committed the wrong thing"**  
→ Don't panic! Talk to your instructor. Git history is hard to truly break.

**"I have merge conflicts"**  
→ Open the conflicted file and look for `<<<<<<`, `=======`, `>>>>>>>`  
→ Edit to keep the right version, save, stage, commit.

---

## Helpful Resources

- 📖 [Happy Git with R](https://happygitwithr.com/) — Jenny Bryan's essential guide for R users
- 🐙 [GitHub Docs](https://docs.github.com) — Official documentation
- 🎒 [GitHub Student Developer Pack](https://education.github.com/pack) — Free pro features with a `.edu` email
- 📺 [GitHub Skills](https://skills.github.com/) — Interactive, hands-on courses
- 💬 [Stack Overflow](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/git) — Community Q&A

---

class: middle

# Hands-On Activity 3

---

.your-turn[

**Fork and Contribute**

1. Go to the course repository (your instructor will share the link)
2. **Fork** the repository to your own GitHub account
3. **Clone** your fork to RStudio
4. Create a new folder called `introductions/` if it doesn't already exist
5. Inside that folder, create a new file: `YOUR-GITHUB-USERNAME.md`
6. Write 2–3 sentences about yourself and your interest in data science
7. Stage, commit (`Add my introduction`), and push
8. On GitHub, open a **Pull Request** to merge your addition into the main repo

⏱️ *Take 5 minutes — ask for help if you get stuck!*

]

---

class: middle

# Wrapping Up

---

## What We Covered

.pull-left[
### Concepts
- ✅ What Git and GitHub are
- ✅ Repositories, commits, branches, forks
- ✅ The pull → stage → commit → push workflow
- ✅ Issues and Pull Requests
]

.pull-right[
### Skills
- ✅ Create a GitHub account and repo
- ✅ Edit files on GitHub.com
- ✅ Clone a repo to RStudio
- ✅ Complete the full Git workflow
- ✅ Fork a repo and open a Pull Request
]

---

## The Golden Rule of Git

.large[
**Commit early, commit often.**
]

--

- Small commits are easier to understand, review, and undo
- Write messages your future self will thank you for
- Push to GitHub at the end of every work session — it's your backup

--

.question[
What questions do you have?
]

---

## Further Reading

- [Happy Git with R](https://happygitwithr.com/) by Jenny Bryan — *the* reference for R + Git
- [Pro Git](https://git-scm.com/book/en/v2) — free, comprehensive Git book
- [GitHub Docs: Getting Started](https://docs.github.com/en/get-started)
- *R for Data Science* Ch. 6: Workflow — [r4ds.had.co.nz](https://r4ds.had.co.nz/workflow-projects.html)

---

# Sources

- Mine Çetinkaya-Rundel's Data Science in a Box ([link](https://datasciencebox.org/))
- Jenny Bryan's [Happy Git with R](https://happygitwithr.com/)
- [GitHub Docs](https://docs.github.com)
- PhD Comics [phdcomics.com](http://phdcomics.com)
